questions.passage: A street name is one of the most ordinary forms of public memory. It is spoken while giving directions, printed on utility bills, entered into delivery apps, and rarely considered as an argument. That ordinariness is precisely why disputes over renaming streets become so intense. A statue announces itself as commemoration; a street sign pretends to be infrastructure. It embeds a judgment about whom the city can ask its residents to repeat without ceremony. The controversy is therefore not merely about history but about the daily grammar of belonging. Defenders of inherited names often describe renaming as erasure. They are not entirely wrong to fear amnesia. Cities that constantly polish away embarrassment risk presenting themselves as innocent. But the opposite danger is equally real: a city may preserve a name so casually that repetition becomes a form of obedience. To keep honoring a slave trader, a colonial governor, or a local benefactor whose wealth rested on exclusion is not simply to remember complexity. It may be to make certain residents rehearse a civic story that has never included them except as obstacles. The strongest renaming efforts avoid the fantasy of purification. They do not pretend that changing a sign repairs the injuries attached to the name. Nor do they imagine that a more admirable name will settle history. Their value lies in making public memory interruptible. A hearing, a plaque, a temporary sign that shows both the old and new names: these devices can turn infrastructure back into argument. They admit that the city is not a museum of settled gratitude but a set of decisions continually inherited by people who did not make them. There is a risk, however, that renaming becomes a ritual of moral speed. Officials may remove a name quickly to avoid harder questions about schools, housing, policing, or land. In that case, the new sign functions like a receipt for virtue. The street appears corrected while the structures that made the old honor plausible remain in place. A politics of names is weakest when it treats language as a substitute for redistribution or repair. Still, symbolism should not be dismissed because it is symbolic. A name is not a sidewalk, but neither is it vapor. It is a small instruction repeated by thousands of mouths. The question is not whether street names matter more than material conditions; they plainly do not. The question is whether a city can change what it repeatedly asks people to say while also admitting that speech is only the beginning of justice. A better practice would make commemoration interruptible without making history disposable. A renamed street might include a small marker explaining the earlier name and the reason for change, not so that old honor continues by other means, but so that the city does not pretend its moral education began yesterday. Such a compromise will disappoint those who want symbolic acts to be either empty theater or complete redemption. Yet public memory rarely offers those clean alternatives. It is made from repeated habits, partial corrections, and explanations that may be ignored by many of the people who pass them. The task is not to find a name innocent enough to end debate. It is to decide what a community should be willing to repeat, and what it should be willing to explain when repetition is interrupted.
questions.stem: The passage suggests that street renaming is ethically significant chiefly because street names:

choices.A: erase prior history whenever they are changed by civic authorities.
choices.B: make public honor part of ordinary repeated speech.
choices.C: matter only when they are attached to famous national figures.
choices.D: solve symbolic conflict more effectively than material reforms can.

explanations.A: The author warns against amnesia, but does not say every change erases history. The preferred approach interrupts honor while explaining what came before.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage’s main point is that a street sign embeds judgment in routine use. The ethical force comes from making residents repeat a public honor as infrastructure.
explanations.C: The argument concerns ordinary repetition, not fame. A local benefactor or governor can matter because residents are asked to say the name repeatedly.
explanations.D: The passage explicitly denies that symbolic change repairs material injuries. It treats naming as meaningful but limited.

questions.stem: The comparison between statues and street signs primarily serves to:

choices.A: show that some commemorations are powerful because they appear merely practical.
choices.B: claim that statues are morally harmless compared with transportation systems.
choices.C: argue that visible monuments should be removed before street names are considered.
choices.D: prove that infrastructure has no symbolic meaning until activists give it one.

explanations.A: Correct. The passage contrasts a statue that announces commemoration with a sign that disguises commemoration as infrastructure. The comparison clarifies why ordinary names can carry quiet authority.
explanations.B: The author does not rank statues as harmless. The point is about the different way street names operate, not about statues lacking moral weight.
explanations.C: No sequence of reforms is proposed. The comparison explains how naming works, not what cities must address first.
explanations.D: The passage says the sign already embeds judgment. Activism may reveal that meaning, but it does not create it from nothing.

questions.stem: Which policy would best fit the author’s preferred approach?

choices.A: Changing disputed names without acknowledging why they were honored in the first place.
choices.B: Keeping inherited names unchanged so that residents never forget civic wrongdoing.
choices.C: Removing all personal names from streets to avoid future disagreement.
choices.D: Renaming a street while marking both the old honor and the reason for revision.

explanations.A: This would risk the polished innocence the passage criticizes. The author wants interruption of honor without pretending the city has no history to explain.
explanations.B: The author rejects passive repetition of honor as a form of obedience. Keeping a name unchanged may preserve memory, but it also continues the civic request to honor it.
explanations.C: The passage does not seek a controversy-free naming system. It accepts that public memory involves judgment and explanation rather than escape from disagreement.
explanations.D: Correct. The passage explicitly favors making commemoration interruptible while avoiding amnesia. A marker that explains both the old name and the change preserves that balance.

questions.stem: The author’s attitude toward symbolic civic acts is best described as:

choices.A: contemptuous because symbols distract from every form of material reform.
choices.B: reverent because symbols can redeem a city from past wrongdoing.
choices.C: indifferent because public memory belongs only to historians.
choices.D: qualified because symbols matter without being sufficient.

explanations.A: The author criticizes treating symbols as everything, but also rejects treating them as vapor. That middle position rules out contempt.
explanations.B: The passage denies that renaming can redeem the city or repair civic injuries by itself. Symbolic acts are limited.
explanations.C: Public memory is presented as a civic practice embedded in daily life, not as a matter reserved for specialists.
explanations.D: Correct. The passage repeatedly balances significance with insufficiency. Street names shape ordinary speech, yet material justice requires more than new signs.

questions.stem: The phrase “daily grammar of belonging” most nearly refers to:

choices.A: the ordinary language by which a city signals whom it honors.
choices.B: the formal legal rules that determine who owns public roads.
choices.C: the private feelings residents have about their ancestral neighborhoods.
choices.D: the technical vocabulary planners use when designing street grids.

explanations.A: Correct. The phrase links ordinary speech to civic membership. Street names are repeated in directions, bills, and apps, so they become part of how belonging is rehearsed.
explanations.B: Ownership rules are not the focus. The passage is concerned with honor and repetition, not property law.
explanations.C: Private feeling may be affected by names, but the phrase refers to public linguistic practice rather than interior memory alone.
explanations.D: Planning terminology would be too narrow and technical. The passage emphasizes everyday use by many residents.

questions.stem: Which discovery would most weaken the passage’s argument?

choices.A: Some people continue using older street names informally after official changes.
choices.B: Residents often learn about local history only after a renaming debate begins.
choices.C: Renaming a street consistently reduces discussion of the injustice at issue.
choices.D: Street renaming requires less funding than major infrastructure repair.

explanations.A: Continued informal usage complicates reform but does not undermine the claim that official repetition carries civic meaning.
explanations.B: This would support the author’s view that renaming can make memory interruptible and visible. Debate becomes a source of historical attention.
explanations.C: Correct. If renaming reliably made injustice less discussable, it would undercut the author’s hope that symbolic change can interrupt memory without producing innocence.
explanations.D: Cost comparisons do not address the core argument. The author already says symbolic acts are not substitutes for material repair.

questions.stem: The author would most likely reject which claim?

choices.A: A public name can be both practical infrastructure and moral statement.
choices.B: Renaming can be worthwhile even when it cannot repair deeper injuries.
choices.C: Old names should go unexplained because explanation preserves honor.
choices.D: Remembering wrongdoing may require changing how honor is repeated.

explanations.A: This is central to the argument. The sign pretends to be infrastructure while carrying judgment.
explanations.B: The author’s qualified support depends on this exact distinction between meaningful interruption and full repair.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage proposes explanation as a way to avoid both amnesia and continued honor. Explaining an old name need not preserve it as an honorific.
explanations.D: The passage supports this view by distinguishing memory from continued obedience to inherited honors.
